label: Uncomplicated head lice without secondary infection
summary: Your symptoms fit head lice, also called pediculosis capitis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated scalp head lice. We did not find secondary skin infection, body or pubic lice, eyelash involvement, or another emergency problem today.
home: Avoid sharing combs, brushes, hats, towels, pillows, or hair items until treatment is complete.
Check close contacts as your clinician recommended.
Wash recently used bedding, hats, and towels according to the plan you were given.
meds: Use lice medicine only as prescribed or recommended, and follow the directions closely.
Do not use extra doses, pesticides, gasoline, kerosene, or home chemicals on the scalp.
Ask before repeating treatment if symptoms continue.
return: Spreading redness, pus, fever, swollen painful skin, or red streaks.
Lice involve eyelashes, eyebrows, body hair, or pubic hair.
Rash, swelling, trouble breathing, or severe irritation after a treatment.
follow: Follow up with primary care, dermatology, or school/occupational health if lice persist, contacts keep getting reinfested, or treatment is not working.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated scalp head lice.
No secondary skin infection, eyelash/eyebrow involvement, body lice, pubic lice/STI pathway, systemic allergic reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, impetigo, abscess, pus, fever, or systemic illness.
Eyelash/eyebrow lice, body lice, pubic lice/STI pathway, treatment toxicity, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: CDC supports head-lice framing, scalp predominance, medication-based treatment, and practical care measures. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated scalp head lice without secondary infection, eyelash/eyebrow involvement, body or pubic lice/STI pathway, toxic treatment exposure, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, immunocompromise, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated scalp head lice
no secondary infection, cellulitis, eyelash lice, pubic lice, or body lice documented
no toxic treatment exposure, pregnancy, pediatric, immunocompromised, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated tinea cruris without cellulitis or immunocompromised host
summary: Your symptoms fit tinea cruris, also called jock itch.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated fungal groin rash. We did not find cellulitis, abscess, genital sores, STI concern, or an immunocompromised-host problem today.
home: Keep the groin area clean and dry.
Change out of sweaty clothing promptly.
Avoid sharing towels or clothing until symptoms are improving.
meds: Use antifungal medicine only as prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use steroid creams on the rash unless your clinician specifically told you to.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, fever, red streaks, or rapidly worsening pain.
Genital sores, penile or vaginal discharge, testicular pain, or STI concern.
Rash spreads widely, keeps returning, or does not improve with the plan.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if symptoms are not improving, recur often, or spread despite the plan.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea cruris or jock itch.
No cellulitis, abscess, genital ulcer/STI pathway, immunocompromised host, extensive or recurrent fungal rash, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, pus, fever, systemic illness, severe pain, rapidly progressive rash, or immunocompromised host.
Genital ulcers, STI concern, scrotal/testicular pain, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, extensive or recurrent fungal rash, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus and CDC support tinea-cruris/jock-itch framing as a fungal groin infection, moisture/friction prevention, and antifungal treatment concepts. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated tinea cruris without cellulitis/abscess, genital ulcer or STI pathway, scrotal/testicular emergency concern, immunocompromise, extensive/recurrent disease, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea cruris or jock itch
no cellulitis, secondary infection, abscess, immunocompromised host, or STI concern documented
no scrotal/testicular, extensive/recurrent, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated tinea versicolor without systemic symptoms
summary: Your symptoms fit tinea versicolor, a superficial fungal skin rash.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated tinea versicolor. We did not find cellulitis, drug-rash warning signs, systemic illness, or immunocompromised-host risk today.
home: Avoid picking or scratching irritated areas.
Protect affected skin from sunburn while the color changes fade.
meds: Use antifungal medicine only as prescribed or recommended.
Skin color can take time to even out after the fungus is controlled.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, fever, red streaks, or worsening pain.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, target-like rash, or facial swelling after a medicine.
Rash becomes widespread, painful, recurrent, or does not improve with the plan.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if symptoms persist, recur often, spread, or the diagnosis is uncertain.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea versicolor.
No secondary infection, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, pus, fever, systemic illness, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, mucosal lesions, or skin peeling/sloughing.
Immunocompromised host, extensive/recurrent rash needing individualized plan, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports tinea-versicolor framing as a common superficial fungal infection causing lighter or darker scaly patches. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated tinea versicolor without infection, drug-rash warning signs, systemic illness, immunocompromise, extensive/recurrent individualized pathway, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea versicolor
no cellulitis, secondary infection, fever, systemic symptoms, or systemic illness documented
no drug-rash, immunocompromised, extensive/recurrent, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated pityriasis rosea without systemic symptoms
summary: Your symptoms fit pityriasis rosea, a common self-limited rash.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated pityriasis rosea. We did not find drug-rash warning signs, infection, pregnancy-related concern, or systemic illness today.
home: Use gentle skin care and avoid harsh soaps or fragranced products.
Keep follow-up if the rash changes or does not improve as expected.
meds: Use itch or skin medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Stop any new product that worsens the rash unless your clinician told you otherwise.
return: Fever, feeling very ill, spreading redness, pus, severe pain, or red streaks.
Mouth sores, eye pain, skin peeling, blistering, target-like lesions, or facial swelling.
Pregnancy, new medication concern, or rash that rapidly worsens or does not follow the expected course.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if symptoms persist, worsen, recur, or the diagnosis is uncertain.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated pityriasis rosea.
No severe cutaneous adverse reaction, mucosal lesions, infection, systemic illness, pregnancy, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Severe cutaneous adverse reaction, mucosal lesions, skin peeling/sloughing, target lesions, cellulitis, abscess, fever, or systemic illness.
Pregnancy, medication reaction concern, immunocompromised host, pediatric pathway, STI/syphilis concern, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports pityriasis-rosea framing, typical herald-patch/scaly-rash course, itching, and resolution over weeks. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated pityriasis rosea without drug-rash warning signs, mucosal involvement, infection, pregnancy, STI mimic concern, systemic illness, immunocompromise, pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated pityriasis rosea
no mucosal lesions, skin peeling, medication reaction, fever, or systemic illness documented
no pregnancy, STI, immunocompromised, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Uncomplicated bruise or contusion without fracture or bleeding risk
summary: Your symptoms fit a bruise or soft-tissue contusion.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated bruise or contusion. We did not find fracture concern, dangerous bleeding risk, neurovascular problem, or abuse concern today.
home: Rest and protect the bruised area.
Use cold packs early if your clinician said they are safe for you.
Raise the area when practical to reduce swelling.
meds: Use pain medicine only as prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Avoid medicines your clinician told you to avoid because of bleeding risk.
return: Severe or worsening pain, numbness, weakness, color change, tight swelling, or trouble using the limb.
Large unexplained bruises, bleeding that will not stop, black stool, fainting, or new bruises without injury.
New concern for fracture, abuse, unsafe home situation, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, occupational health, or orthopedics if pain, swelling, or function is not improving.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated bruise or soft-tissue contusion.
No fracture/dislocation concern, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, anticoagulation/bleeding disorder, non-accidental trauma concern, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture, dislocation, open wound, severe pain, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, inability to use limb, or high-energy trauma.
Anticoagulation, bleeding disorder, unexplained bruising, abuse/assault concern, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports bruise/contusion framing, expected pain/swelling/color-change course, and simple supportive care. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated bruise/contusion without fracture/dislocation concern, compartment syndrome, neurovascular compromise, open wound, high-energy mechanism, anticoagulation/bleeding disorder, unexplained bruising or abuse concern, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated bruise or soft-tissue contusion
no fracture, dislocation, neurovascular compromise, bleeding disorder, or anticoagulation documented
no open wound, high-energy mechanism, abuse concern, pregnancy, pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway
